mcqs.question: Which of the following is an example of a discrete random variable?

options.text: The number of customers arriving at a store in an hour
The exact temperature of a liquid
The time it takes to complete a task
The weight of a randomly selected package

mcqs.explanation: The number of customers is countable and finite, making it discrete. The other options represent continuous measurements.

mcqs.question: What defines a continuous random variable?

options.text: It can only take on integer values
It has a countable number of possible values
It can take on any value within a given interval
Its outcomes are always whole numbers

mcqs.explanation: A continuous random variable can take on any value in an interval (e.g., height, weight, time), unlike discrete variables which have countable outcomes.

mcqs.question: Which statement is true about the probability mass function (PMF) of a discrete random variable?

options.text: It gives the probability that the variable equals a specific value
It is used to find probabilities for intervals of values
It must integrate to 1 over all possible values
It is defined for continuous random variables

mcqs.explanation: The PMF, p(x) = P(X=x), gives the exact probability for each discrete value. Continuous variables use probability density functions (PDFs).

mcqs.question: For a continuous random variable, the probability that it equals any specific value is always:

options.text: Greater than 0
Equal to 1
Less than 0
Equal to 0

mcqs.explanation: Since continuous variables have infinitely many possible values in any interval, P(X=x) = 0 for any exact point x. Probabilities are calculated over intervals.

mcqs.question: What is the key difference in how probabilities are calculated for discrete vs. continuous random variables?

options.text: Discrete variables use PMFs, continuous variables use PDFs
Discrete variables calculate probabilities over intervals, continuous variables calculate probabilities for exact values
Discrete variables have countable outcomes, continuous variables have uncountable outcomes
Discrete variables are used for counting processes, continuous variables are used for measurement processes

mcqs.explanation: Discrete random variables use PMFs to calculate probabilities for exact values, while continuous random variables use PDFs to calculate probabilities over intervals.
